prompt: RANK-ORDER, most-to-least acute, the specific buyer sub-segments feeling 'enterprise security review is blocking my deal' pain MOST acutely in mid-2026. Segment by: (a) what the AI-native startup SELLS (agentic coding, AI SOC/security, healthcare AI, fintech/payments AI, legal AI, AI customer-support, AI data/RAG infra), (b) stage (pre-seed/seed/Series-A/Series-B), and (c) the deal-size band stalled in review ($50-100k, $100-500k, $500k+). For each top segment cite specific 2025-2026 X posts (verbatim, with @handle + date) or named companies. State which ONE segment a fixed-fee $750 signed Readiness Report should beachhead on, and your confidence. If the evidence is thin for a segment, say so explicitly. Cite every claim.

On the BUYER side (the enterprise whose security team gates the deal), which industries' vendor-security reviewers are now explicitly demanding AI-AGENT-specific evidence (not just SOC2) from their AI vendors in 2026 - finance, healthcare, government, insurance, big-tech? Quote reviewers/CISOs/security engineers verbatim (@handle + date) or cite named enterprise security questionnaires/standards that added AI-agent questions. What exact artifacts does a reviewer say would let them mark an AI vendor 'cleared' (standards mapping, signed evidence, scope statement, named human sign-off)? This defines product requirements - be concrete, list the artifacts, and cite each.

Give REAL free-to-paid conversion benchmarks for comparable bottoms-up dev-tool and security products (Snyk, Socket, Semgrep, Aikido, Gitleaks/Trufflehog, Vanta free tools, Cyphrex free tier, Wiz free trials). For each: published or credibly-reported free->paid conversion rate, free->paid TIME lag, and the single trigger that flips a free user to paying. Cite sources with URLs/handles+dates. Then state what these benchmarks imply for a funnel of free Scan -> $750 signed Readiness Report -> $299/$999/mo continuous. Give numbers, not adjectives; flag where data is anecdotal vs. hard.

What specific MOMENT converts a free security/compliance 'scan' user into a paying buyer in 2026 - a failing finding, an enterprise reviewer asking for proof, a deal deadline, a regulatory date, a board ask? Find verbatim 2025-2026 X posts (@handle+date) and write-ups of founders describing the exact moment they paid for security/compliance proof. Also: which free-tier designs CONVERT vs. which CANNIBALIZE (e.g., free scan that shows problems but withholds the signed report vs. giving everything away). Recommend the free-Scan gating that maximizes conversion to the $750 report, with cited evidence.

SUPPLY-SIDE economics: can credible named co-signers be sourced to co-sign an AI-agent security attestation at ~$1,500-$3,000 per attestation in 2026? Research going rates and availability for: fractional/virtual CISOs (vCISO), independent CISA/CISSP-credentialed auditors, ex-SOC2 auditors moonlighting, security expert networks (GLG/Catalant-style), and boutique vCISO firms. Give concrete hourly/day/engagement rates and how many hours a single attestation co-sign would plausibly take. Name specific firms/marketplaces and cite rates. Conclude whether the $25,000 Reviewed Attestation tier's co-signer cost is viable at $1.5-3k/attestation, with margin math and confidence.

What are the LIABILITY and reputational barriers to a named human putting their signature on a third-party 'AI agent security readiness attestation' for a startup vendor? Research: professional/E&O (errors & omissions) insurance expectations for auditors and vCISOs, real cases of auditors facing liability for sign-offs, and what protections (scope-limiting language, indemnification, 'readiness not certification' framing, insurance) a co-signer would demand before lending their name to an Ed25519-signed report. Quote practitioners (@handle+date) or cite professional-body guidance. State the minimum legal/insurance scaffolding kolm must offer to recruit co-signers; cite each point.

Is EU AI Act Article 12 (record-keeping / automatic logging for high-risk AI) specifically driving BUDGET ALLOCATION NOW (mid-2026) ahead of the Aug 2 2026 milestone, or is it still theoretical to buyers? Distinguish Art.12 logging obligations from the GPAI obligations and the high-risk timeline. Find 2026 evidence of companies line-iteming spend or RFPs citing Art.12/logging specifically (quote @handle+date or cite procurement docs/vendor pages). Clarify: do Series-A AI-native startups selling to enterprises actually fall in scope (as providers of high-risk systems or via enterprise customers' obligations), or is the urgency mostly customer-driven pressure? Give a NOW-vs-theoretical verdict with confidence and cite everything.

Beyond EU AI Act: is the Colorado AI Act (effective 2026), ISO/IEC 42001 demand, and NIST AI RMF adoption creating time-boxed BUYING of AI-security/compliance proof in 2026, or wait-and-see? What are buyers actually purchasing to comply (ISO 42001 certification, AI governance tooling, attestations, logging)? Quote 2026 practitioners (@handle+date) and cite vendor/news sources. Identify which single regulatory date is the sharpest budget-unlock for a fixed-fee AI security readiness product, and whether to anchor kolm's urgency messaging on EU AI Act Aug 2 2026 vs. an alternative. Cite each claim; flag hype vs. real spend.

Where do ENTERPRISE security reviewers / vendor-risk teams / CISOs congregate and how does a NEW third-party attestation FORMAT get socialized and accepted into their review process in 2026 (CISO Slack/Discord communities, vendor-risk forums, SafeBase/Whistic/Conveyor trust-exchange networks, Vanta/Drata partner marketplaces, RSA/BSides, procurement platforms)? Quote reviewers (@handle+date) or cite the specific channels/marketplaces. What makes a reviewer trust and reuse a new attestation type rather than ignore it? List the concrete distribution surfaces kolm should target on the REVIEWER side, ranked, with citations.

What is the cheapest, fastest channel to the FIRST 100 paying customers for a founder-led, $750 fixed-fee security-readiness wedge sold to Series-A AI startups in 2026? Evaluate with evidence: YC/accelerator batches and founder Slacks, AI-founder X communities, security-questionnaire-automation marketplaces, Vanta/Drata/MCP-registry integrations and partner listings, design-partner/logo programs, and cold founder outreach. For each channel cite a comparable tool that won early traction there (with source). Rank channels by cost-per-first-100 and speed, name the top 2, and state confidence. Cite everything; flag speculation.

Stress-test this EXACT pricing ladder against real 2026 stated willingness-to-pay and competitor moves: free Scan, $750 one-time signed Readiness Report, $299/mo and $999/mo continuous, $25,000 flat Reviewed Attestation (named co-signer), +$10,000 Deep Red-Team. Find verbatim 2026 evidence (@handle+date or cited pages) of what AI startups actually pay / call a 'no-brainer' / call 'too expensive' at each of these price points. Specifically probe: (1) is $750 a frictionless card-swipe or should it be lower/higher; (2) does the $299->$999/mo step make sense; (3) is the $999/mo -> $25,000 jump a dangerous gap that needs an intermediate tier; (4) Van-Westendorp-style: what's 'too cheap to trust' and 'too expensive' for a signed readiness report. Give specific dollar evidence per point, a verdict on each anchor (hold / adjust + to what), and confidence.

Is $25,000 flat for a named-human co-signed AI-agent security ATTESTATION credibly priced in 2026, benchmarked against: a full SOC2 Type II (commonly $15-50k+ all-in), an external pen-test ($10-30k typical), an ISO 42001 certification, and a fractional-CISO retainer? And is a +$10,000 Deep Red-Team add-on priced right vs. the 2026 AI/LLM red-team and pentest market? Cite specific 2026 price points (URLs, vendor pages, @handle+date). State what a buyer expects to RECEIVE for $25k (scope, named signer, liability, standards coverage) to consider it fair, and whether $25k should hold, rise, or fall. Cite each comparison.

What have these competitors shipped, priced, or announced in the LAST ~45 DAYS (roughly late-April through June 2026): Cyphrex (pricing/feature/positioning changes to its $49/$199/$599 continuous tiers and signed reports), Vanta (any 'AI trust', 'AI agent', or attestation product), Lakera / Check Point (post-acquisition agent-security moves), Drata, SafeBase, Whistic, Conveyor? Include funding rounds. Quote announcements/posts (@handle+date) and cite. For each, state whether the move strengthens or threatens kolm's wedge (fixed-fee, Ed25519-signed, named co-signer, standards-mapped readiness report). Be current and specific; if you can't confirm recency, say so.

Search hard for ANY product launched or announced in 2026 that does kolm's EXACT wedge: a cryptographically SIGNED 'AI agent security readiness' or 'agent attestation' report, mapped to SOC2/ISO 42001/NIST AI RMF/EU AI Act/OWASP/MITRE ATLAS, sold as a fixed fee, optionally with a named human co-signer. Name every direct or near-direct clone you can find (beyond Cyphrex), with launch date, pricing, and @handle/URL. If a true clone exists, quantify how close it is. If none exists, state that explicitly and characterize how defensible the gap is. Cite everything.

Trademark and brand-clearance scan for the name 'kolm' / 'kolm.ai' in the software & security category. Check for conflicting or confusingly-similar marks in USPTO (and note EUIPO) in Nice classes 9 (software) and 42 (SaaS/security services): existing 'Kolm', 'Kohlm', 'Qualm', 'Column', 'Kolmogorov', or similar registered/pending marks, and any active companies/products using 'Kolm' in tech/security/finance. Note domain and social-handle conflicts for 'kolm'. Assess likelihood-of-confusion and descriptiveness risk, and whether 'kolm' is registrable for this category. Cite specific filings/URLs. This is a risk flag, not legal advice - be explicit about uncertainty and recommend whether a formal clearance/filing is warranted before public launch.

Adversarial: why would a QUALIFIED Series-A AI buyer (deal stalled in security review) NOT buy a $750 third-party signed Readiness Report, or distrust it? Enumerate the real objections and substitute behaviors in 2026: build their own trust center, use Vanta/Drata questionnaire-automation AI, commission a pentest, write a security whitepaper, push back on the reviewer, or wait for full SOC2. For each substitute, cite 2026 evidence (@handle+date or URL), give its rough cost/time, and the switching friction away from it toward kolm. Quote any skepticism about small-vendor attestations verbatim. Conclude with the top 3 objections kolm's positioning/product must neutralize, ranked, with citations.
